title: Session identity: resolve/generate session_id + current-session pointer (agent-agnostic)
description: Define what a session is and how the appender (bd-timew-ahp) obtains a session_id, agent-agnostically. Claude sessions: session_id from the Claude Code hook payload (stable across compaction; /clear is graceful and starts a new id). Human/manual sessions: --session-id optional with auto-generation; a current-session pointer (machine + controlling tty keyed) lets a human's later 'bd-timew stop X' reuse the id generated by 'start X'. --session-id NOT mandatory (the CLI cannot enforce it; mandatory is a UX regression). MINIMAL pre-merge scope: just session_id resolution + the pointer. NO claimfiles, NO lifecycle sentinels (those are post-merge, operational-layer).
notes: Liveness facts (verified): SessionEnd does NOT fire on crash; /clear is graceful (new id); compaction keeps id; no PID in hook payload (payload has session_id, cwd, transcript_path).
close_reason: Implemented session-id resolution + current-session pointer; 86 tests passing, live-verified

title: bd-timew CLI: re-wire start/stop/switch/status/active/report onto the JSONL backend
description: Replace timew calls in the bd-timew CLI with the JSONL writer + aggregator. Preserve the command surface (start/stop/switch/status/report) for muscle memory. Add 'active' subcommand to show open intervals across ALL sessions (the explicit multi-active view that timew cannot give). Document the behavior change in CHANGELOG.md.

title: Schema v1 + appender: write start/stop/cancel/correction JSONL events with ULID interval IDs
description: Implement the JSONL event writer per the schema in bd-timew-nfr. Append-only, POSIX O_APPEND so concurrent writers interleave cleanly. ULIDs (or UUIDv7) for interval IDs. Session ID resolved from $BEADS_SESSION env var (or a session-init that generates one). Tags snapshot taken from bd labels at event time. Storage: <project_root>/.bd-timew/sessions/<session-id>.jsonl.
notes: Per-event schema fields (v1): event, v, interval (ULID), session_id, ts, and provenance fields carried inline (denormalized at write time): group_id (nullable; agent-agnostic — a group of humans or a swarm of agents), actor (free-form, nullable — what the actor IS, e.g. human/claude/coordinator), role (free-form, nullable — what the actor DOES, e.g. dev/architect/TPO/planner). actor and role are orthogonal axes kept separate so neither is unrecoverable. No enum enforcement — the config layer (blh) interprets values. RATIONALE: the log is the immutable authoritative record; provenance omitted now cannot be backfilled. actor is populatable pre-merge (human vs supervised); group_id stays null until a coordinator sets it, so pre-merge logs are fully classifiable with zero backfill.

title: Aggregator + summary cache: walk per-session JSONL, pair intervals, apply corrections, emit reports
description: Reader/aggregator for the JSONL event log. Pairs start/stop by interval ID; applies latest correction per interval; caps/drops cancelled or stale-open intervals. Writes summary cache with watermark for incremental subsequent runs. Reports group by bead / billing label / session, supporting --mode machine|wallclock per bd-timew-nfr.
notes: Summary cache must store paired intervals at the finest provenance granularity (interval + group_id x actor x role) so the config-driven aggregation policy (see blh) is applied at REPORT time, not baked into the cache — keeps all modes derivable from one log. Pre-merge reaping is LOG-DRIVEN stale-open detection (cap/close orphaned open intervals at last-good ts); no claimfiles/sentinels pre-merge (those are post-merge, operational-layer).

title: Design: per-session JSONL event log for concurrent timetracking (replace timew single-active backend)
description:
## Motivating failure mode (from J121 operator session, 2026-05-27)

timew (and bd-timew on top of it) has a single global active interval. Running two
parallel Claude sessions against the same project clobbered each other's intervals:
`bd-timew start J121-wne` in one session was auto-ended when the other session
ran `bd-timew start J121-obv`; the first session's later `bd-timew stop`
inadvertently closed the second session's interval. Time attribution scrambled
across sessions. Mike accepted this as a known issue at the time (deferred), but
the structural problem is timew's single-active model -- there is no clean fix
inside timew.

## Alternatives considered

1. **Custom fields in the Dolt DB.** Heavy. Couples timetracking schema evolution
   to bd. bd's data model is issue-centric; time-interval lists do not fit cleanly.
   Either extends bd itself (entangles release cadence) or carries a separate Dolt
   table (Dolt overhead without the in-bd win). Rejected.

2. **Single JSON file with in-place edits.** Re-creates the timew problem:
   read-modify-write under two concurrent writers is exactly the race that broke
   timew. Rejected.

3. **Append-only JSONL event log (RECOMMENDED).** Append writes of < PIPE_BUF
   bytes with O_APPEND are atomic at the OS level (POSIX), so concurrent writers
   interleave events cleanly without locks or corruption. Read aggregation walks
   the log; trivially cheap until the log is huge. Optional per-session split file
   sidesteps git merge conflicts. Summary-cache file with a watermark accelerates
   repeated queries (mirrors bd's own JSONL export pattern). This is the right
   shape because it directly solves the property (atomic concurrent writes) that
   the other file-based options lack.

4. **Adopt another existing tool.** Watson, org-mode timeclock, Hamster, hledger
   time: all single-active-task models or otherwise the same constraint as timew,
   OR (hledger) heavy adoption cost without solving the multi-active question.
   Rejected.

## Recommended design

### Event schema (v1)

```jsonl
{"v":1,"event":"start","interval":"<ulid>","bead":"J121-e6w2","ts":"2026-06-01T13:45:00-05:00","session":"op-2026-05-27","tags":["case:07_04 ...","client:..."]}
{"v":1,"event":"stop","interval":"<ulid>","ts":"2026-06-01T14:30:00-05:00"}
{"v":1,"event":"cancel","interval":"<ulid>","ts":"..."}
{"v":1,"event":"correction","interval":"<ulid>","start":"...","stop":"..."}
```

- **Interval IDs (ULID or UUIDv7)**: sortable + unique; pair starts with stops
  unambiguously even when concurrent intervals overlap.
- **`session` field**: lets aggregation distinguish parallel sessions; required
  for the machine-vs-wallclock reporting modes.
- **`tags` snapshot**: carry the billing tuple inline so historical lookups
  do not need to re-resolve labels (which may have evolved).
- **`cancel` event**: for abandoned intervals (session crash). Aggregator caps
  or drops intervals with no matching stop after some threshold.
- **`correction` event**: for backfill -- append a new event referencing an
  existing interval with corrected start/stop. Aggregator uses latest correction
  per interval. Preserves append-only.

### Storage location

Project-rooted `<project_root>/.bd-timew/` (NOT inside `.beads/`). Works
identically for embedded-Dolt and shared-server bd projects -- the timetracking
JSONL has no dependency on bd's storage layer. Fallback for projects without a
clear root: `~/.local/share/bd-timew/<project-name>/`.

Per-session file (`<dir>/sessions/<session-id>.jsonl`) inside the storage dir
avoids git merge conflicts when concurrent machines push.

### Aggregator

Walk per-session files, pair start/stop (by interval ID), apply latest
correction per interval, drop or cap cancelled/orphaned. Summary cache at
`<dir>/summary.json` with a `watermark` (last-processed event ts per session
file). Incremental update on every run.

### CLI surface (bd-timew, retains command names)

```
bd-timew start <bead>     # append start event with current ts + $BEADS_SESSION
bd-timew stop  <bead>     # append stop event for this session's open interval on <bead>
bd-timew switch <bead>    # stop current + start new (atomic at session level)
bd-timew status           # this session's open intervals
bd-timew active           # ALL open intervals across all sessions
bd-timew report --since=YYYY-MM-DD --group-by=bead|case|client|session [--mode machine|wallclock]
```

### One deliberate design choice: reporting mode default

Two faithful interpretations of concurrent-session time data:

- **Machine hours (sum across sessions)**: a wall-clock minute with two
  sessions active counts twice. Reflects compute / work-product output.
- **Wallclock attended (union)**: a wall-clock minute counts once regardless
  of session count. Matches traditional person-hour billing.

Both are derivable from the same JSONL log via the aggregator's `--mode` flag.
Decide which is the default before the first report goes out. Recommendation:
**wallclock for default billing reports; machine-mode opt-in**, since most
billing models charge person-hours not compute-instances.

## Pitfalls + mitigations

- Open interval (start without stop) when session crashes: aggregator caps at
  threshold (default 4h?) and flags for manual review via a `cancel` event.
- Clock skew across machines: ISO timestamps with TZ; aggregator robust to
  out-of-order events.
- Git merge conflicts on concurrent appends from multiple machines: per-session
  file split.
- Editing past intervals: `correction` event, never edit past events.
- Migration from existing timew data: one-shot import script reads timew
  export into JSONL events (separate task).

title: Define version-increment policy for the package (when + how to bump)
description: Decide upfront when to increment the Python package version (pyproject.toml) and to what. Mechanical to execute, but the rule should be fixed before implementation lands so bumps are consistent. Likely shape: SemVer; minor bump per validated layer (ahp schema+appender, zzl aggregator, 9hn CLI cutover); MAJOR bump (or version reset under the new package name) at cutover/rename. Depends on the rename decision because a rename = the major version event (new package name, possible reset). NOTE: package SemVer is DISTINCT from the JSONL schema 'v' field (ahp) — they version different things; keep them separate.
design: DECISION (2026-06-02): SemVer; stay in 0.x through the JSONL rewrite. Bump MINOR as each validated layer bead closes: ahp -> 0.3.0, zzl -> 0.4.0, 9hn (cutover + rename to bd-track) -> 0.5.0; subsequent layers continue minor. PATCH for intra-layer fixes. Reserve 1.0.0 until the JSONL event format + CLI surface have survived real billing cycles (1.0 = 'billed real money off it and trust it'), NOT at the rename — the new package name carries the rebrand signal, the version need not jump. The bump is mechanical: edit pyproject [project].version when closing each layer bead. Package SemVer is DISTINCT from the JSONL schema 'v' field (ahp).
notes: Upstream dependency: rename decision. Should be in place before the cutover (9hn) ships a version; related to 9hn rather than hard-blocking it to avoid graph rigidity.
close_reason: Policy set: 0.x minor-per-layer, 1.0.0 reserved for post-billing-proof

title: Decide new package + executable name (drop 'timew' reference)
description: With timewarrior being removed as the backend, the 'timew' in 'bd-timew' becomes a misleading reference to a backend we no longer use. Decide a replacement for both the Python package and the core executable. Keep the bd- prefix (signals beads-companion coupling; still shells out to bd). Candidates (design centers on intervals/spans of tracked work): bd-track (RECOMMENDED — clearest intent, reads as 'bd-track start <bead>', aligns with the time-tracking skill name), bd-time (minimal, accurate), bd-span (accurate to the interval model, less obvious). EXECUTE the rename atomically at the CLI cutover (bd-timew-9hn) — already a breaking change + natural version event. COST: touches every wrapper (/start /stop /switch /status, time-tracking + work-queue skills), install.sh, systemd templates, pyproject.toml.
design: DECISION (2026-06-02): rename package + executable to 'bd-track'. Rationale: clearest intent ('bd-track start <bead>'), aligns with the time-tracking skill name, keeps the bd- prefix. EXECUTION deferred to the 9hn CLI cutover (atomic with the backend swap — already a breaking change + natural version event). Blast radius to handle at cutover: pyproject (name + [project.scripts]), executable, install.sh, systemd templates, and every wrapper/skill (/start /stop /switch /status, time-tracking + work-queue skills).
notes: Decide the NAME now; defer EXECUTION to the cutover. A rename is the biggest version event, so this blocks the versioning-policy decision.
close_reason: Decided: rename to bd-track; execution deferred to 9hn cutover

title: Evaluate bd-timew <-> claude-config integration (merge vs depend-with-boundary)
description: Decide how bd-timew relates to the claude-config project. The operational/authoritative cut maps onto a code boundary: bd-timew could stay a standalone installable owning the authoritative log + schema + aggregator + agent-agnostic CLI, while claude-config builds the coordinator layer (claimfiles, sentinels, hooks, swarm semantics) ON TOP of it and sets the provenance fields. CONSIDERATIONS: (1) agent-agnostic constraint — a solo human shouldn't pull in swarm/sandbox scaffolding; (2) VM-visibility — across containers/Firecracker VMs, operational state needs coordinator-mediated channels; (3) Lua-hook perf — compels INTEGRATION, which 'depend' satisfies; does not by itself compel a wholesale merge; (4) cold/hot path split aligns (hot=operational state in claude-config; cold=log aggregation in bd-timew).
notes: PRIMARY merge motivation is CONTINGENT: coordinating work across two beads instances is painful — but if an effective cross-instance workaround is found, or both projects move onto the shared ~/.beads, that motivation dissolves and the case tilts toward 'depend, don't merge'. Flag the contingency so it doesn't silently become load-bearing.

title: Design the aggregation-policy configuration mechanism (merge/aggregation semantics per provenance axis)
description: Two faithful interpretations of concurrent-session time data (see bd-timew-nfr). Recommendation in the design: wallclock for default billing reports; --mode machine opt-in. Confirm + document the choice before any time report is sent for billing. Both modes remain implementable from the same JSONL log; only the default flips.
notes: Reframed from 'pick a default (wallclock vs machine)' to designing the config MECHANISM; specific policies stay user-configurable and are deferred (semantics still in-flight). Mechanism shape: (a) partition intervals into groups by a configurable key over provenance axes (group_id / actor / role); (b) within-group combine op (union | sum); (c) across-group combine op. Config specifies keys + ops. Decidable now; unblocks zzl's cache granularity. NOTE: avoid hardcoding 'swarm=union, human=sum' — express it as configured policy over the axes.

title: Shared-server projects: confirm project-rooted .bd-timew/ vs ~/.local/share/bd-timew/ fallback
description: The design defaults to <project_root>/.bd-timew/ for storage. For projects without a clear project root (rare in current setup), fallback is ~/.local/share/bd-timew/<project-name>/. Confirm this is the right rule for shared-server-mode projects (the bd-timew project itself is one such). The shared bd-state location does NOT determine the timetracking-storage location -- timetracking is decoupled from bd's storage layer.
design: DECISION (2026-06-02): authoritative JSONL event log location FOLLOWS the active beads dir — <beads_dir>/bd-timew/sessions/<session-id>.jsonl. Shared ~/.beads work converges into ~/.beads/bd-timew/ (centralized billing, zero registry); project-local .beads keeps its log local. Rides beads' git/dolt sync (per-session-file split avoids cross-machine merge conflicts). Fallback for server-mode / no local .beads: ~/.local/share/bd-timew/<project-id>/. Reject ~/.cache (XDG-disposable). DISTINCT from sfk's current-session pointer, which is machine-local operational state in ~/.local/state and NEVER synced. ahp impl note: ensure <beads_dir>/bd-timew/ is not caught by beads' own .gitignore so the log rides sync.
notes: SCOPE: the AUTHORITATIVE LOG location only. Recommendation: follow find_beads_dir() (companion-to-beads; rides existing sync; work under shared ~/.beads converges into one dir = centralized billing, zero registry), injectable/coordinator-aware, with ~/.local/share/bd-timew/<project>/ fallback for server-mode (no local .beads dir). Reject ~/.cache (XDG-disposable; billing data is not). OUT OF SCOPE / POST-MERGE: operational-state (claims/sentinels) location — that's coordinator-owned, machine-local, never synced (see operational-layer).
close_reason: Decided: log follows the beads dir (shared ~/.beads converges); ~/.local/share fallback for server-mode

title: Migration: import existing timew data into the JSONL event log
description: One-shot migration script: read existing timew interval export (timew export), convert each interval to a start+stop event pair in JSONL, preserve original tags and start/stop timestamps. Run once at cutover; archive timew data after verification.

title: Shared ADO PR webhook listener -> bd-timew dispatcher for cross-project bead/status automation
description:
## Goal

Stand up a single local HTTP receiver fronted by a Cloudflare tunnel that accepts Azure DevOps Pull Request webhook events and dispatches them to per-project handlers. The dispatch layer lives inside `bd-timew` so the per-project bead-state transitions and post-hooks can reuse the same project lookup, billing tuple, and queue plumbing that `bd-timew` already maintains.

Architecture is one shared listener for all projects (J121, claude-config, any future projects with their own bd instance), with project-name-based routing rather than one server per project. The listener is dumb; bd-timew owns the logic.

## Architecture

```
ADO project (e.g. J121-pipelines)
    -> Service Hook: PR created/updated/merged/abandoned
    -> POST to https://j121-webhooks.<your-domain> (Cloudflare tunnel)
    -> cloudflared tunnel -> localhost:8765 on dev workstation
    -> j121-pr-webhook-listener.service (Python http.server or FastAPI)
    -> dispatches to: bd-timew webhook-dispatch <project> <event-type> <payload-json>
    -> bd-timew resolves project from sidecar (~/.config/bd-timew/repos.yaml),
       finds linked bead from PR metadata, executes status transition + optional per-bead post-hook script
```

The listener is project-agnostic. Project routing happens via:

1. ADO repo name (from the webhook payload `resource.repository.name`) -> sidecar `repos[].project_name` or new `repos[].ado_repos[]` array
2. PR branch name -> bead ID regex (`J121-[a-z0-9]+`, etc.) -> bd lookup in the resolved project's instance
3. Fallback: PR description scan for `<prefix>-<id>` patterns matching any project's `issue_prefix`

## Server options (decision: Cloudflare tunnel)

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloudflare tunnel** (recommended) | Free, persistent named tunnels survive reboots, no rate-limit tier surprises, terminates TLS for you, can route via your own DNS | Requires CF account + domain (already have both); slightly more first-time setup than ngrok |
| **ngrok** | Easiest first-run | Free tier rotates URLs on each restart, paid tier needed for stable URL; bandwidth caps |
| **Tailscale Funnel** | Built-in if already on Tailscale | Quota limits; ties webhook routing to Tailscale identity |
| **Self-hosted reverse proxy + public IP** | No third-party dependency | Requires actual public IP and DNS; significant security surface |

Cloudflare tunnel chosen for the combination of free, persistent, and TLS-terminated. Named-tunnel pattern: `cloudflared tunnel create bd-timew-webhooks`, then a config file pointing `<sub>.<domain>` -> `http://localhost:8765`, then `systemctl --user enable --now cloudflared-bd-timew-webhooks.service`.

## ADO webhook setup

Per ADO project (J121 first, others as they come online):

1. Project Settings -> Service Hooks -> Create subscription
2. Service: **Web Hooks**
3. Trigger events (one subscription per event we want to react to, or a single "PR updated" subscription with handler-side filtering):
   - `Pull request created`
   - `Pull request updated`
   - `Pull request merged` (the high-value one for closing review-pr beads)
4. Filters: repo = `J121-pipelines` (or whatever); optionally filter by target branch
5. Settings:
   - URL: `https://j121-webhooks.<your-domain>/ado/pr`
   - Resource details to send: All
   - HTTP headers: `X-Webhook-Token: <shared-secret>`  -- for HMAC-style auth without full HMAC
   - Basic authentication: optional second factor
6. Test the subscription using ADO's "Test" button before saving (it sends a sample payload).

Subscriptions are configured per-event-type, so a typical project needs 3-4 subscriptions (created, updated, merged, abandoned). All POST to the same URL; handler dispatches on `eventType`.

## Security concerns and mitigations

| Concern | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| **Tunnel exposes localhost to public internet** | Bind listener to `127.0.0.1` only; tunnel terminates locally. Never bind 0.0.0.0. |
| **Anyone who learns the URL can POST** | Require a shared-secret header (`X-Webhook-Token`) on every request; reject without it. Use a long random token stored in `pass`, sourced via `.envrc` for the listener systemd unit. |
| **Replay attacks** | ADO doesn't sign payloads natively; the shared-secret alone is the auth. Acceptable for this use case (low blast radius -- worst case is unauthorized bead-state transitions, recoverable). If we want HMAC, ADO supports custom headers but not request signing -- would need a proxy layer. |
| **Payload injection / command exec** | The dispatcher only calls `bd-timew webhook-dispatch <project> <event> <payload-file>` with a payload written to a temp file. The bd-timew subcommand parses JSON safely and never shells out with payload content. Per-bead post-hook scripts are user-controlled; they should treat payload fields as untrusted. |
| **Schema drift on payload** | Handler logs unrecognized event types or missing fields, returns 204 (so ADO doesn't retry indefinitely), and surfaces via a desktop notification (`wsl-notify-send`) for triage. |
| **Tunnel credential rotation** | cloudflared tunnel uses a long-lived service token; documented as part of the install runbook. Annual rotation is fine for this risk level. |
| **bd-timew sidecar contains routing config** | `~/.config/bd-timew/repos.yaml` is already a sensitive routing doc (Dolt server passes etc.). New `ado_repos:` field is non-secret. No change to access model. |

## Proposed implementation

### Listener (new)

- `~/.local/src/bd-timew-webhooks/listener.py` -- ~50 lines, stdlib `http.server`
- Reads `X-Webhook-Token` from env (`PR_WEBHOOK_TOKEN`)
- Routes `/ado/pr` to ADO handler; future `/github/pr`, `/gitlab/pr` slots already named
- Dispatches by writing the JSON payload to `/run/user/<uid>/bd-timew-webhook-<unixmillis>.json` and exec'ing `bd-timew webhook-dispatch ado <payload-file>`
- Returns `204 No Content` on accepted dispatch, `400` on bad payload, `401` on bad token

### bd-timew additions (this is the bulk of the work)

- New subcommand `bd-timew webhook-dispatch <provider> <payload-file>`
- Provider-specific parsers translate raw payload -> normalized event:
  ```
  {
    "provider": "ado",
    "event": "pr.merged" | "pr.created" | "pr.updated" | "pr.abandoned",
    "repo": "J121-pipelines",
    "pr_id": 1811,
    "source_branch": "mhaynes/feat/docs-restructure",
    "target_branch": "main",
    "title": "...",
    "description": "...",
    "url": "https://dev.azure.com/..."
  }
  ```
- Project resolution: scan `repos:` in sidecar for a match on `ado_repos:` array (NEW field) containing `<org>/<project>/<repo>`. Falls back to substring match on `repos[].path` against PR description for cross-project tickets.
- Bead resolution within the project: regex on `source_branch` for the project's `issue_prefix`; fallback to PR description scan
- Action: status transition per event (`pr.created` -> `review-pr`; `pr.merged` -> `closed`; `pr.abandoned` -> `open`). Configurable per-project in sidecar via `repos[].pr_status_map:`
- Post-hook dispatch: if `~/.config/bd-timew/pr-hooks.d/<project>/<bead-id>.sh` exists, exec it with the normalized payload as `$1`. Lets us wire per-bead automation (e.g., the 8 worker-doc courtesy comments triggered when J121-fmnb closes) without changing the core dispatcher.
- Logging: append to `~/.local/state/bd-timew/webhook.log` (rotated daily)
- Errors: don't blow up; log + desktop notify

### systemd units (new)

Two user units:

1. `bd-timew-webhook-listener.service` -- runs the Python listener, depends on the token being set
2. `cloudflared-bd-timew-webhooks.service` -- runs the named tunnel

Both `WantedBy=default.target` so they autostart on user login. Listener has `Restart=on-failure` with backoff.

### Sidecar schema additions

New per-repo field in `~/.config/bd-timew/repos.yaml`:

```yaml
repos:
- path: /home/hactar/Source/J121
  ...
  ado_repos:
    - boldorange/J121/J121-pipelines
    - boldorange/J121/portal-stabilization
    - boldorange/J121/SmartStore_PlugIns
  pr_status_map:
    created: review-pr
    merged: closed
    abandoned: open
```

The `ado_repos` field is what the dispatcher uses to route incoming webhooks to the right project's bd instance. `pr_status_map` allows per-project override of the default transitions.

## Out of scope (for this bead)

- GitHub PR webhook support (slot is reserved in the dispatcher; implement later)
- Bidirectional sync (e.g., bd state -> Jira comment posting). The dispatcher is one-way.
- Multi-machine deployment (this is single-workstation tooling for now)
- HMAC payload signing (ADO doesn't support natively; revisit if a CI/CD vendor switch happens)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Listener accepts a valid signed ADO webhook payload and returns 204
- [ ] `bd-timew webhook-dispatch` correctly identifies the target project from a real ADO `pr.merged` payload for J121-pipelines
- [ ] Bead status transitions on `pr.created` (-> review-pr) and `pr.merged` (-> closed) for at least J121-1nx and J121-fmnb as test cases
- [ ] Per-bead post-hook script fires after status transition with the normalized payload in env
- [ ] Cloudflare tunnel + listener systemd units restart cleanly across reboots
- [ ] Rejection of unsigned / wrong-token requests is verified
- [ ] Setup documented in the bd-timew README (or a new docs/webhooks.md)

## Implementation phases (suggested)

1. **bd-timew dispatch subcommand + sidecar schema** -- pure code, testable without the listener
2. **Local listener** -- raw POST testing via curl, no tunnel yet
3. **Cloudflare tunnel + systemd units** -- networking layer
4. **ADO subscription setup** -- per-project; J121 first
5. **End-to-end test against a real PR** -- best with a throwaway test PR
6. **Per-bead post-hook example** -- wire the J121-fmnb -> 8 courtesy comments trigger as the proof-of-concept post-hook

## References

- Cloudflare tunnel docs: https://developers.cloudflare.com/cloudflare-one/connections/connect-networks/
- ADO Service Hooks: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/service-hooks/
- bd-timew sidecar schema: `~/.config/bd-timew/repos.yaml`
- Related J121-side concrete use case: 8 worker-doc courtesy comments queued under `~/Source/J121/tasks/project/claude-autonomous/J121-fmnb-docs-restructure/` waiting on PR 1811 merge

title: Coordinator operational layer: claimfiles + lifecycle sentinels + liveness hooks (post-merge)
description: Holds the deferred Primitive #1 (session claims) and Primitive #2 (session lifecycle) design from docs/dev/timetracking-architecture.06022026.md. Operational state is coordinator-owned, machine-local, ephemeral, rebuildable from the log, NEVER synced. Claims = a per-machine SET (not a lock) of open intervals, materialized as claimfiles (cache, never billing truth). Sentinels = liveness via timestamp freshness (Claude: transcript_path mtime; human: idle), reaped lazily + on SessionStart sweep. Claimfiles-as-gates are allowed for COORDINATION decisions (perf), provided a stale/missing gate degrades to re-deriving from the log. Correct design depends on claude-config's coordinator/visibility model + hook architecture — hence deferred until integration is decided.
notes: Live claims = claimfiles INTERSECT live sentinels; an orphaned claimfile triggers closing that session's open interval at last-good ts. Reject git-refs for this state (refs are built to sync; this state must not propagate). Location: coordinator-owned (e.g. ~/.local/state/bd-timew/<project-id>/), injectable per swarm/coordinator.

value: posix O_APPEND atomicity: a write() system call with O_APPEND set, of size < PIPE_BUF (typically 4096 bytes), is guaranteed atomic by POSIX. Concurrent writers appending to the same file do not interleave bytes from different writes. This is the structural property that makes the JSONL event-log approach for bd-timew (bd-timew-nfr) concurrency-safe without locks -- the property timew lacks because its single global active-interval state requires read-modify-write.

timew single-active failure mode under parallel claude sessions: two concurrent sessions calling bd-timew start clobbered each other's intervals (J121 operator session, 2026-05-27). Session A's start was auto-ended by Session B's start; Session A's later stop inadvertently closed Session B's interval. This is the originating incident for bd-timew-nfr (replace timew backend with append-only JSONL event log per session).

beads-dir-envvar-contamination-also-bites-here: BEADS_DIR + BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD set in J121 .envrc leak into Claude's persistent shell. To run bd in bd-timew (or any other project) from such a contaminated shell, prefix with 'env -u BEADS_DIR -u BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD'. bd then auto-discovers project from cwd correctly. Wrapping in a shell function (BD() { env -u BEADS_DIR -u BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD bd "$@"; }) is convenient for sessions doing multiple bd calls. Original pitfall documented in global CLAUDE.md.
